scenarios.name: meta/insecure-ai-client
scenarios.title: Securing the AI Client
scenarios.prTitle: feat: add retry and caching to AI client
scenarios.bugDescription: Hardcoded API key, PII in logs, retry without backoff

findings.title: Hardcoded secret or credential
findings.description: A secret, password, API key, or token appears to be hardcoded in source code or configuration.
findings.suggestion: Use environment variables or a secrets manager. Never commit secrets to version control.

findings.title: Overly broad exception catch
findings.description: Catching a generic exception type hides specific errors and makes debugging harder. May silently swallow important failures.
findings.suggestion: Catch specific exception types. If catching broadly, at minimum log the exception.

findings.title: Retry loop without backoff causes thundering herd
findings.description: The retry logic (lines 50-84) retries immediately without any delay or exponential backoff. This will hammer the API on transient failures, likely triggering rate-limiting or temporary bans. The comment "no backoff needed" is incorrect for transient API failures—backoff is essential to avoid overwhelming the service during degradation.
findings.suggestion: Add exponential backoff: `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, Math.pow(2, retries) * 100))` before retrying, or use a library like `p-retry`.

findings.title: Untyped catch block prevents selective retry logic
findings.description: The bare `catch` block (line 80-82) swallows all errors without distinguishing between retryable (429, 500-level) and non-retryable (400, 401, 404) failures. This causes the code to waste retries on authentication errors or malformed requests, and masks the root cause of failures during debugging.
findings.suggestion: Catch as `catch (err)`, check error status/type, and only retry on transient errors: `if (err instanceof Anthropic.APIError && (err.status >= 500 || err.status === 429)) { retries++; } else { throw; }`

findings.title: Debug logging exposes sensitive prompt content
findings.description: Line 31 logs the full prompt to console unconditionally. If this prompt contains user data, PII, or proprietary information, it will be visible in logs and potentially exposed in log aggregation systems. The comment acknowledges this is for troubleshooting but provides no conditional guard.
findings.suggestion: Remove the debug log or gate it behind an explicit `debug` flag: `if (opts.debug) console.log('Sending prompt...', params.prompt.slice(0, 100))`

findings.title: Unsanitized cacheKey used in file path construction
findings.description: The `params.cacheKey` value is interpolated directly into a file path via `join(this.cacheDir, \`${params.cacheKey}.json\`)`. If a caller passes a cacheKey like `../../etc/passwd` or `../../../home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys`, the join() call will resolve outside the intended cache directory. An attacker who controls `cacheKey` can read arbitrary files (via the readFile branch) or overwrite arbitrary files (via the writeFile branch), including writing attacker-controlled JSON content to sensitive paths.
findings.suggestion: Validate that the resolved path stays within cacheDir before use: `const resolved = join(this.cacheDir, \`${params.cacheKey}.json\`); if (!resolved.startsWith(path.resolve(this.cacheDir) + path.sep)) throw new Error('Invalid cacheKey');`. Additionally, whitelist cacheKey to alphanumeric characters and hyphens: `/^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/`.

findings.title: Cache bypass in live mode ignores cached results unconditionally
findings.description: When `this.live` is true, the cache is never read before making an API call, even though a successful response is always written to cache. This means in live mode, every call hits the API regardless of whether a fresh cached result exists. The asymmetry (write always, read only when !live) makes the cache write-only in live mode, wasting API spend and making retries more expensive.
findings.suggestion: Separate the 'use cache instead of API' flag from the 'also write to cache' flag, or check the cache first in live mode and only call the API if the cache is stale/missing.

scenarios.name: meta/broken-deterministic-gate
scenarios.title: Refactoring the Gate
scenarios.prTitle: refactor: simplify deterministic gate pattern matching
scenarios.bugDescription: Weakened SQL check, timing-unsafe compare, SSRF rule disabled

findings.title: SQL query built with string concatenation or interpolation
findings.description: SQL query uses string interpolation or concatenation with user-controlled values instead of parameterized queries.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements.

findings.title: Async callback in forEach (fire-and-forget)
findings.description: forEach does not await async callbacks. Promises execute concurrently without error handling, causing race conditions and silent failures.
findings.suggestion: Replace with for...of loop with await, or use Promise.all(array.map(async ...)).

findings.title: Potential null/undefined dereference
findings.description: A value that may be null or undefined is accessed without a null check. This can cause runtime crashes.
findings.suggestion: Add a null check before accessing the value, or use optional chaining (?.).

findings.title: Self-assignment or self-comparison
findings.description: A variable is compared or assigned to itself. This is almost always a bug (copy-paste error or wrong variable name).
findings.suggestion: Check for typos in variable names.

findings.title: Potential SSRF: URL opened without validation
findings.description: A URL is opened/fetched using user-controlled input without allowlist validation. This enables Server-Side Request Forgery. (3 occurrences in this file)
findings.suggestion: Validate the URL against an allowlist of permitted hosts/schemes before fetching.

findings.title: Dangerous HTTP security header configuration
findings.description: Security headers are set to values that disable protections (e.g., X-Frame-Options: ALLOWALL, CSP: unsafe-inline). (3 occurrences in this file)
findings.suggestion: Use restrictive security header values. X-Frame-Options should be DENY or SAMEORIGIN.

findings.title: Method call on potentially nil/null value
findings.description: A method is called on a value that could be nil/null/undefined based on surrounding context. (2 occurrences in this file)
findings.suggestion: Add a nil/null check or use safe navigation operator (&. in Ruby, ?. in JS/TS).

scenarios.name: meta/hollow-test-suite
scenarios.title: Hollow Test Suite
scenarios.prTitle: test: add comprehensive pipeline layer tests
scenarios.bugDescription: 15 tests mocking their own subjects — zero behavioral assertions

findings.title: 4/15 tests are circular (mock-on-mock)
findings.description: Lines 76-90 test only that mocked objects have expected layer numbers (0, 1, 2, 3). These are tautological: the mock setup on lines 6-34 explicitly assigns these values, so the assertions merely confirm the mock setup itself. No actual layer logic is validated.

findings.title: Mock inconsistency: AIQuickScan.gate.proceed = false prevents L3 execution
findings.description: The AIQuickScan mock returns `gate: { proceed: false, ... }` on line 23. This causes the pipeline to skip AIDeepReview execution (line 73 test expects this). However, real pipeline behavior depends on actual gate logic. The mock's hardcoded `proceed: false` doesn't test the conditional execution path when `proceed: true`, leaving that scenario untested and creating false confidence.
findings.suggestion: Add a separate test that mocks AIQuickScan with `proceed: true` to verify AIDeepReview *is* called when the gate permits. Or use parameterized tests to cover both gate states.

findings.title: Layer:start event count assertion is fragile and incomplete
findings.description: Line 101 expects `layer:start` to be called 3 times, but the pipeline has 4 layers (L0–L3, see lines 6–34 mocks). If AIDeepReview is skipped (which line 73 confirms it is), only 3 layers run, making this assertion coincidentally correct but misleading. It doesn't validate the *correct* set of layers actually ran—just that 3 events fired.
findings.suggestion: Verify which specific layers emitted events by checking event payloads (e.g., `expect(handler).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(1, expect.objectContaining({ layer: 0 }))`). Or test the conditional execution explicitly: one test with gate open (all 4 layers), one with gate closed (3 layers).

findings.title: No validation that report.layers array contains expected layer results
findings.description: Line 120 checks that `report.layers.length > 0` but doesn't validate layer order, layer IDs, or findings structure. The test passes if *any* layers are present, not the correct ones. Combined with the gate-closed mock, this cannot detect if L3 incorrectly appears in the report.
findings.suggestion: Assert exact layer count and check layer identity: `expect(report.layers.map(l => l.layer)).toEqual([0, 1, 2])`. Add a separate test for the gate-open path to verify L3 appears.

findings.title: AIDeepReview gate-stop test contradicts its own mock setup
findings.description: The test 'does not call AIDeepReview when gate says stop' relies on AIQuickScan returning `gate.proceed: false` — which is exactly what the global mock already returns for every test. This means the AIDeepReview layer should never be called in ANY test, including 'calls AIQuickScan.analyze' and the other positive tests. If the pipeline actually respects the gate (proceed: false → skip L3), then the test named 'calls AIDeepReview...' (if it exists in the truncated section) would silently pass even when AIDeepReview is broken, because the mock prevents it from ever being reached. Conversely, the gate-stop assertion is vacuously true and proves nothing about the conditional logic.
findings.suggestion: Override the AIQuickScan mock locally in tests that need L3 to execute: use `vi.spyOn(aiQuickScan, 'analyze').mockResolvedValueOnce({ ..., gate: { proceed: true, ... } })` before calling pipeline.run in those tests. The global mock should default to `proceed: true` so the positive path is exercised by default.

findings.title: previousLayers field passed as empty array may mask accumulation bugs
findings.description: Every `pipeline.run` call supplies `previousLayers: []`. If the pipeline accumulates layer results into the context object passed to subsequent layers (i.e., each layer receives the results of prior layers), passing an empty array bypasses that path entirely. A bug where the pipeline fails to forward prior results to later layers would never be caught.
findings.suggestion: At least one test should supply a non-empty `previousLayers` value and assert that the downstream layer's `analyze` call receives it correctly, verifying the accumulation/forwarding logic.

findings.title: All tests verify mock calls, never pipeline output — broken pipeline logic passes invisibly
findings.description: Every test asserts only that `analyze` was called (or not called) on mock instances. The pipeline's actual responsibilities — aggregating layer results into a report, computing totals, merging findings, setting overall risk/status — are never verified against real values. If `ReviewPipeline.run()` returned `null`, an empty object, or silently swallowed errors after calling the mocks, every test would still pass. The 100% line coverage claim is accurate but meaningless: lines are executed, but correctness of the output is unverified.
findings.suggestion: Assert on the return value of `pipeline.run()`. At minimum: check that `report.layers` has the expected length, that `report.findings` is an array, and that `report.metrics.totalCostUsd` equals the sum of mock layer costs (0 + 0 + 0.02 + 0 = 0.02 when L3 is skipped, 0.42 when it runs). This catches any aggregation bug the call-count assertions cannot.

findings.title: 'gate says stop' test cannot prove L3 is actually skipped — the mock setup makes AIDeepReview unreachable regardless
findings.description: The test titled 'does not call AIDeepReview when gate says stop' is structurally indistinguishable from a test where the pipeline ignores the gate entirely. Because `AIQuickScan.analyze` is globally mocked to return `gate: { proceed: false }` in `beforeEach`, AIDeepReview would also not be called if the pipeline had a bug such as an off-by-one that dropped the last layer, or if the pipeline crashed after L2 and swallowed the error. The negative assertion (`not.toHaveBeenCalled`) passes in all three scenarios: correct gate logic, incorrect gate logic with a crash, and incorrect gate logic that simply never reaches L3. A complementary test with `gate.proceed: true` that confirms L3 *is* called is required to make the negative test meaningful — without it the security-relevant early-exit path is unverified.
findings.suggestion: Add a paired positive test: override the AIQuickScan mock for that single test to return `gate: { proceed: true }`, run the pipeline, and assert `aiDeepReview.analyze` was called. Only with both the positive and negative cases does the gate boundary have genuine test coverage.

scenarios.name: standalone/payment-service
scenarios.title: Payment Service
scenarios.bugDescription: SQL injection via f-string, card data in logs, weak JWT secret

findings.title: SQL query built with string concatenation or interpolation
findings.description: SQL query uses string interpolation or concatenation with user-controlled values instead of parameterized queries. (2 occurrences in this file)
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements.

findings.title: Full card number and CVV logged in plaintext
findings.description: Line 16 logs the complete card number and line 16 logs sensitive payment data. This violates PCI-DSS compliance and exposes cardholder data in audit logs, creating a significant security breach and regulatory violation.
findings.suggestion: Never log full card numbers or CVV. Log only masked data like last 4 digits: `logger.info(f"Processing card ****{request.card_number[-4:]} for amount {request.amount}")`

findings.title: Hardcoded secret key in source code
findings.description: Line 11 contains a hardcoded SECRET_KEY. This credential is exposed in version control and accessible to anyone with repository access, compromising authentication security.
findings.suggestion: Load SECRET_KEY from environment variables: `SECRET_KEY = os.getenv('SECRET_KEY')` and document in .env.example

findings.title: Second SQL injection in INSERT statement
findings.description: Line 31 also uses string interpolation in INSERT statement with user-controlled amount and card_number data. Although Layer 1 flagged line 24, this second query on line 31 is equally vulnerable and will execute attacker-supplied SQL.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries: `cursor.execute("INSERT INTO charges (customer_id, amount, card_last4) VALUES (?, ?, ?)", (customer_id, request.amount, request.card_number[-4:]))`

findings.title: Unguarded string slice on card_number crashes on short input
findings.description: request.card_number[-4:] is evaluated before any length check. If card_number is an empty string or fewer than 4 characters, Python silently returns a shorter string — but more critically, if card_number is None (possible if the Pydantic field is ever made Optional in a future model change, or if validation is bypassed), this raises AttributeError and crashes the request handler mid-transaction, leaving the database connection unclosed and potentially the charge in an inconsistent committed/uncommitted state. Even with current validation, a 1–3 character card_number passes Pydantic's str type and reaches this line, producing misleading audit data with no error surfaced to the caller.
findings.suggestion: Validate card_number length explicitly (e.g., min_length=16 in the Pydantic field). Use card_last4 = request.card_number[-4:] only after asserting len(request.card_number) >= 4, and wrap DB operations in a try/finally to ensure conn.close() is always called.

findings.title: Database connection not closed on exception paths
findings.description: The database connection is opened at line 20 but only closed at line 34 in the happy path. If the HTTPException at line 27 is raised (customer not found), or if any other exception occurs during cursor.execute or conn.commit, conn.close() is never called. This leaks database connections on every 404 response and any runtime error.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the database operations in a try/finally block (or use a context manager `with sqlite3.connect(...) as conn:`) to guarantee conn.close() is always called regardless of the execution path.
